past_medical_history: Neonatal bacterial meningitis (pneumococcal) at 3 weeks of age, complicated by hydrocephalus requiring ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement
VP shunt revision at age 8 due to catheter malposition
Mild chronic nonprogressive encephalopathy attributed to neonatal meningitis sequelae
Spastic left hemiparesis since infancy, stable and non-progressive
Cervical arachnoid cyst discovered at age 16, monitored conservatively
Posterior cervical decompression and cyst fenestration at age 18, complicated by transient right arm weakness (white cord syndrome), which resolved over 6 months with rehabilitation
family_history: Mother with well-controlled epilepsy (generalized tonic-clonic seizures in adolescence, seizure-free on valproate for 15 years)
Maternal uncle with a history of febrile seizures in childhood
No family history of brain tumors or neurodegenerative diseases

social_history.occupation: Part-time warehouse assistant with accommodations for motor limitations
social_history.education: Completed high school with special education support, reading at 10th grade level
social_history.living_situation: Lives with parents in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Limited by spasticity; walks with ankle-foot orthosis, no regular exercise program

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cooperative but mildly anxious. Speech is fluent with occasional word-finding pauses. Attention span intact for bedside testing. Montreal Cognitive Assessment score 24/30, losing points on delayed recall (3/5), trail-making (1 error), and phonemic fluency (9 words).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three trigeminal divisions bilaterally. Mild left lower facial droop, consistent with upper motor neuron pattern (longstanding). Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: shoulder abduction 4/5, elbow flexion 4+/5, grip strength 4/5, finger abduction 4-/5. Left lower extremity: hip flexion 4/5, knee extension 4+/5, ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Increased tone in left upper and lower extremities with spastic catch, more prominent distally. No pronator drift on the right.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense mildly reduced at left ankle compared to right.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+. Left biceps 3+, triceps 3+, brachioradialis 3+, patellar 3+ with crossed adductor response, Achilles 3+ with sustained clonus (4 beats). Left Babinski sign present (extensor plantar response). Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact on the right without dysmetria. Left finger-to-nose mildly impaired by spasticity rather than true cerebellar dysmetria. Heel-shin testing intact on the right, mildly impaired on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates with left ankle-foot orthosis. Left leg circumduction pattern consistent with spastic hemiparesis. Stance phase shortened on the left. Unable to perform tandem gait due to baseline motor deficit. Romberg test negative.
neurological_exam.additional: VP shunt palpable in right parietal region; reservoir fills and refills appropriately. Well-healed posterior cervical midline surgical scar. No meningeal signs.

patient.chief_complaint: Three episodes of staring and unresponsiveness with lip-smacking over the past two months, the most recent occurring yesterday with a witnessed generalized convulsion.
patient.history_present_illness: This 24-year-old right-handed male with a complex neurological history including neonatal pneumococcal meningitis, VP shunt placement, chronic left spastic hemiparesis, and prior cervical spinal surgery presents to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. His mother reports that over the past two months, the patient has experienced at least three episodes of behavioral arrest during which he appears to stare blankly, becomes unresponsive to verbal commands, and exhibits rhythmic lip-smacking and fumbling hand movements with his right hand lasting approximately 60 to 90 seconds each. After these episodes, he appears confused and disoriented for 10 to 15 minutes, often unable to recall the event. The family initially attributed these episodes to daydreaming or inattention related to his baseline cognitive profile. Yesterday evening, while watching television, the patient was observed to suddenly stop speaking mid-sentence, develop a fixed stare to the right, and begin lip-smacking. This progressed over approximately 30 seconds to bilateral arm stiffening followed by generalized rhythmic jerking of all four extremities lasting approximately two minutes. He was incontinent of urine during the event. Post-ictally, he was confused, somnolent, and had new right arm weakness that gradually improved over two hours. Emergency medical services were called and the patient was transported to the emergency department. He had no prior seizure history despite his complex neurological background. Over the preceding weeks, the patient has also complained of intermittent headaches, which his family attributed to stress at work, and a vague sensation of an unpleasant smell that no one else could perceive, occurring several times per week for approximately one month. He denies recent head trauma, fever, or illness. He has not started any new medications, and there have been no changes to his baclofen regimen. His mother notes that he has appeared more fatigued and irritable over the past several weeks, with subtle decline in his already limited reading ability.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric but at the lower limit of normal
background.symmetry: Mild amplitude asymmetry with slightly lower voltage over the left hemisphere
background.reactivity: Posterior dominant rhythm attenuates with eye opening

findings.morphology: Moderate amplitude, biphasic, with after-going slow wave; morphology is suggestive but not definitively epileptiform
findings.clinical_correlation: Right anterior temporal sharp transients of uncertain clinical significance; could represent epileptiform discharges or benign variants

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs lasting 2-5 seconds, seen multiple times
findings.clinical_correlation: Intermittent right temporal slowing may indicate underlying structural or functional abnormality

findings.morphology: Increased amplitude of beta and mu frequencies over right parietal region
findings.clinical_correlation: Breach rhythm consistent with prior surgical history (VP shunt). Should not be mistaken for epileptiform activity.

artifacts.impact: Mild; intermittently obscures temporal sharp transients

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: 3 minutes of adequate effort. No definite activation of epileptiform discharges. Mild bilateral symmetric slowing during procedure, returning to baseline within 1 minute.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response. Normal photic driving at 8-14 Hz.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to (1) occasional right anterior temporal sharp transients of uncertain significance, and (2) intermittent right temporal slowing. Breach rhythm over the right parietal region is noted and likely related to prior craniotomy. The sharp transients are morphologically suspicious for epileptiform discharges but do not meet definitive criteria on this routine recording. Prolonged or sleep-deprived EEG monitoring is recommended for further characterization.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. No sleep was captured. Muscle artifact intermittently limited temporal chain interpretation.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring with sleep capture to better characterize temporal sharp transients
Consider sleep-deprived EEG if prolonged monitoring is not immediately available

findings.size: Right hippocampus measures 2.8 cm3 vs left 3.6 cm3 on volumetric analysis

signal_characteristics.T2: Equivocal mild hyperintensity in the right hippocampus, could represent early sclerosis or artifact from partial volume averaging
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle increased signal in right hippocampal body, difficult to distinguish from artifact
signal_characteristics.T1: Mild volume loss of right hippocampus compared to left, more apparent on coronal cuts

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense rim with central CSF signal

findings.mass_effect: None; ex vacuo dilatation of adjacent left lateral ventricle

findings.location: Right parietal approach, tip in right lateral ventricle
Periventricular and subcortical white matter, bilaterally
findings.size: Few scattered punctate foci, largest 4 mm

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.8 cm3 (below 10th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: Mild left cerebral hemisphere volume loss consistent with prior insult (neonatal meningitis sequelae)
No restricted diffusion on DWI sequences
Prior posterior cervical surgical changes noted at C2-C4 level with stable post-surgical appearance
mri.impression: 1. Subtle right hippocampal volume loss with equivocal T2/FLAIR signal abnormality, which may represent early mesial temporal sclerosis but cannot be confirmed on this study alone. Recommend epilepsy protocol MRI with thin coronal cuts through the temporal lobes for further evaluation. 2. Left frontoparietal encephalomalacia consistent with known sequelae of neonatal meningitis. 3. VP shunt in appropriate position. 4. Scattered non-specific periventricular and subcortical white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, likely gliotic changes from prior meningitis. No evidence of active demyelinating disease.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Moderate — volumetric asymmetry is suggestive but signal changes are equivocal
Possible — asymmetry alone may be within population variation

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Dual pathology (remote meningitis injury plus hippocampal sclerosis)
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Should be considered given the clinical history of neonatal CNS infection

recommended_actions: Epilepsy protocol MRI with 3T magnet and thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes
FDG-PET to evaluate for temporal lobe hypometabolism
Correlate with EEG findings for lateralization

ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG. No cardiac abnormalities identified.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG helps exclude cardiac arrhythmia as a cause of episodic loss of consciousness.

basic_metabolic_panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely stress response or post-ictal. Not in diabetic range.

thyroid_panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated TSH. Could indicate subclinical hypothyroidism. May lower seizure threshold in some patients, but unlikely to be the primary cause of seizures.

post_ictal_markers.clinical_significance: Elevated prolactin drawn approximately 45 minutes post-ictally. Elevation above 2x baseline within 20 minutes supports a generalized tonic-clonic or focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure. This level is moderately elevated but was drawn outside the optimal 10-20 minute window.
Elevated CK consistent with recent generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity with sustained muscle contraction.
Mildly elevated lactate, consistent with recent seizure activity. Expected to normalize within hours.

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results show post-ictal changes including elevated prolactin, CK, and lactate, supporting a recent generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Basic metabolic panel is largely normal with mildly elevated glucose likely attributable to physiologic stress. Mildly elevated TSH with normal free T4 suggests subclinical hypothyroidism, which warrants monitoring but is unlikely to be the primary seizure etiology. Toxicology screen is negative. No metabolic derangement identified as a precipitating cause for seizures.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8-9 Hz alpha rhythm, attenuates with eye opening
background.symmetry: Mild amplitude asymmetry, lower over left hemisphere (stable from prior study)
background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep transients captured including vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes

findings.location: Right anterior temporal (F8/T4), maximum at T4
findings.frequency: Frequent during drowsiness and NREM sleep (approximately 15-20 per hour of sleep), rare during wakefulness (1-2 per hour)
findings.morphology: Sharp waves with phase reversal at T4, amplitude 80-120 microvolts, with consistent after-going slow wave. Definitively epileptiform.
findings.state: Predominantly drowsiness and NREM sleep stages 1 and 2
findings.clinical_correlation: Definitive right anterior temporal epileptiform discharges consistent with a right temporal epileptic focus

findings.location: Right temporal onset (F8/T4), with secondary spread to right posterior temporal and right parasagittal regions
findings.frequency: One subclinical electrographic seizure captured during sleep, duration 38 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta activity at 5-6 Hz evolving in frequency and distribution, with post-ictal regional attenuation
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical right temporal seizure captured on prolonged monitoring. Confirms right temporal seizure onset.

findings.morphology: Irregular delta activity (1-3 Hz) in right temporal region, consistent with temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA)
findings.clinical_correlation: TIRDA is a highly specific marker for temporal lobe epilepsy and supports the right temporal focus.

activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Sleep was captured after sleep deprivation protocol. Significant activation of epileptiform discharges during drowsiness and sleep.

output.impression: Abnormal prolonged video-EEG monitoring (48 hours). Definitive right anterior temporal epileptiform discharges (sharp waves) with clear activation during drowsiness and sleep. One subclinical electrographic seizure of right temporal origin was captured. TIRDA present. These findings confirm a right temporal epileptic focus and are consistent with focal epilepsy of right temporal lobe origin. Clinical semiology of the patient's habitual events (behavioral arrest, oroalimentary automatisms, right hand fumbling) is concordant with right temporal lobe seizure onset.
output.limitations: No habitual clinical seizure was captured during monitoring, although one subclinical electrographic seizure was recorded.
recommended_actions: Correlate with high-resolution MRI findings for concordance
Consider FDG-PET if surgical evaluation is planned

findings.size: Right hippocampus volume 2.6 cm3 (5th percentile); left hippocampus 3.7 cm3 (50th percentile)

signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Definite hyperintensity in right hippocampal body and tail on thin coronal cuts
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Confirmed hyperintensity in right hippocampus, clearly distinct from artifact
signal_characteristics.T1_volumetric: Right hippocampal atrophy with loss of internal architecture and digitations

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.6 cm3 (5th percentile for age and sex)
volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 3.7 cm3 (50th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No temporal lobe mass or tumor
Temporal horn of right lateral ventricle mildly dilated (ex vacuo from hippocampal volume loss)
output.impression: High-resolution epilepsy protocol MRI confirms right mesial temporal sclerosis with definitive hippocampal atrophy and T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. Hippocampal volumetric asymmetry index of 30% is strongly lateralizing. Left frontoparietal encephalomalacia is stable and likely represents dual pathology from neonatal meningitis. No additional epileptogenic lesion identified.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High — definitive findings on epilepsy protocol

recommended_actions: Correlate with EEG findings for concordance assessment
Neuropsychological testing to assess lateralized temporal lobe dysfunction
Discuss surgical candidacy at epilepsy conference if medically refractory

neuropsychological_assessment.clinical_significance: Reduced verbal learning and recall, lateralizing to dominant (left) temporal lobe or bilateral temporal dysfunction
Mildly impaired naming, could reflect left temporal dysfunction or baseline developmental factors
Reduced phonemic fluency, may relate to left frontal encephalomalacia or developmental history
Reduced cognitive flexibility, likely multifactorial (developmental and structural)

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile shows a pattern of reduced verbal memory and naming relative to preserved visual memory and visuospatial skills. While these findings are partially lateralizing to the left (dominant) hemisphere, the pattern is complicated by the known left frontoparietal encephalomalacia from neonatal meningitis. The relative preservation of visual memory suggests that the right temporal lobe retains adequate function. Overall, the neuropsychological data are consistent with bilateral cerebral dysfunction of differing etiologies (left hemisphere structural injury plus right temporal epileptic focus) and would support right temporal surgical candidacy provided Wada testing or fMRI lateralization confirms adequate contralateral memory reserve.

findings.location: Right temporal lobe, most pronounced in mesial and anterior temporal structures

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Reduced FDG uptake in right mesial temporal structures (SUVr 0.72 compared to contralateral 0.95)
signal_characteristics.extent: Extending to right anterior temporal neocortex and right inferior temporal gyrus

findings.location: Left frontoparietal region corresponding to known encephalomalacia

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Reduced FDG uptake in left frontoparietal cortex (expected finding given structural lesion)

additional_observations: Remainder of cortical FDG uptake is within normal limits
No hypermetabolic focus to suggest ongoing ictal activity at time of scan
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates right temporal hypometabolism centered on the mesial temporal structures, concordant with the EEG lateralization and MRI findings of right mesial temporal sclerosis. Left frontoparietal hypometabolism corresponds to the known structural lesion. No unexpected areas of hypometabolism or hypermetabolism.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epileptic focus (interictal hypometabolism)
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High — concordant with MRI and EEG

recommended_actions: Multimodal concordance review at epilepsy surgery conference
Consider ictal SPECT if non-invasive data remain discordant

antiepileptic_drug_levels.value: Not applicable — patient not yet on antiepileptic medication
antiepileptic_drug_levels.clinical_significance: Patient is AED-naive. This is the first presentation with seizures. No drug levels to assess.

output.interpretation: Patient is not currently on any antiepileptic medication. This represents a new-onset seizure presentation. AED therapy should be initiated based on clinical judgment and the results of the diagnostic workup.

output.query: temporal lobe epilepsy treatment first-line antiepileptic drugs mesial temporal sclerosis

results.title: Practice guideline update summary: Efficacy and tolerability of the new antiepileptic drugs I and II
results.authors: Kanner AM, Ashman E, Gloss D, et al.
results.summary: Level A evidence for lamotrigine and levetiracetam as initial monotherapy for focal epilepsy in adults. Both show favorable efficacy and tolerability profiles.

results.title: Surgical versus medical treatment for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy
results.authors: Engel J Jr, McDermott MP, Wiebe S, et al.
results.summary: Early surgical intervention for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy is superior to continued medical therapy. 73% of surgical patients achieved seizure freedom versus 0% of medical patients in the intention-to-treat analysis.

results.title: Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis: ILAE consensus definition
results.authors: Blumcke I, Thom M, Aronica E, et al.
results.summary: Defines MTS-HS as a specific clinicopathological entity. Recommends comprehensive presurgical evaluation including video-EEG, high-resolution MRI, and neuropsychological testing. Anterior temporal lobectomy yields 60-80% long-term seizure freedom rates.

output.summary: Current evidence supports lamotrigine or levetiracetam as first-line monotherapy for newly diagnosed focal epilepsy. For temporal lobe epilepsy with mesial temporal sclerosis, early referral for surgical evaluation is recommended if seizures are not controlled with the first or second appropriate AED. Anterior temporal lobectomy in well-selected patients yields seizure freedom rates of 60-80%.

interactions: Baclofen + Levetiracetam: Additive CNS depression. Monitor for increased sedation, drowsiness, and cognitive dulling. Consider starting levetiracetam at a lower dose and titrating slowly.
warnings: Levetiracetam may cause behavioral side effects including irritability, agitation, and depression, particularly in patients with pre-existing cognitive impairment or neuropsychiatric history
Monitor for mood changes given patient's baseline mild encephalopathy
output.formulary_status: Formulary — generic available, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily with slow titration (fewer behavioral side effects but slow titration schedule)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily (effective for focal epilepsy but more drug interactions and hyponatremia risk)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily (well-tolerated, fewer interactions, but more expensive)

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily with titration per standard schedule
interactions: No significant interactions with current medications (baclofen, omeprazole, acetaminophen)
Lamotrigine metabolism is not significantly affected by omeprazole or baclofen
warnings: Serious dermatologic reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN) — risk minimized by slow titration
Must titrate slowly: 25 mg daily for 2 weeks, then 50 mg daily for 2 weeks, then increase by 50 mg every 1-2 weeks to target dose of 150-300 mg/day
Not ideal if rapid seizure control is needed due to slow titration schedule
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (faster titration, immediate therapeutic levels)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily (moderate titration speed, well-tolerated)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy of right temporal lobe origin with mesial temporal sclerosis, likely secondary to neonatal meningitis (dual pathology)

differential.diagnosis: Focal epilepsy — right temporal lobe (mesial temporal sclerosis)
differential.key_distinguishing: Stereotyped episodes of behavioral arrest with oroalimentary automatisms, right temporal EEG focus, right hippocampal sclerosis on MRI, concordant PET hypometabolism

differential.diagnosis: VP shunt malfunction with raised intracranial pressure
differential.key_distinguishing: Headaches could suggest shunt dysfunction, but shunt reservoir fills appropriately, no papilledema, no acute hydrocephalus on imaging
Semiology is typical of temporal lobe seizures (not atypical), post-ictal prolactin elevation supports epileptic seizure, EEG captures electrographic seizure
Mildly elevated TSH with normal free T4 is unlikely to cause seizures; subclinical hypothyroidism rarely lowers seizure threshold significantly

differential.diagnosis: Recurrent cervical myelopathy or white cord syndrome
differential.key_distinguishing: Prior white cord syndrome resolved; current symptoms (episodes of altered awareness with automatisms) are not consistent with spinal cord pathology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right anterior temporal sharp transients and intermittent temporal slowing

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural lesion or hippocampal sclerosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle right hippocampal volume asymmetry with equivocal signal changes; left frontoparietal encephalomalacia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, thyroid panel, prolactin, and toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Post-ictal elevations in prolactin, CK, and lactate; mildly elevated TSH; negative toxicology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac arrhythmia as cause of episodes
Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events and confirm epileptic focus
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Definitive right temporal epileptiform discharges and subclinical electrographic seizure

optimal_actions.action: Request epilepsy protocol MRI with thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed right mesial temporal sclerosis with 30% hippocampal volumetric asymmetry

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating antiepileptic therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam-baclofen additive CNS depression; lamotrigine has fewer interactions but slower titration

optimal_actions.action: Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug (levetiracetam or lamotrigine)
Search medical literature for temporal lobe epilepsy management and surgical outcomes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting early surgical evaluation for drug-resistant TLE with MTS

optimal_actions.action: Consider FDG-PET for presurgical evaluation concordance
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right temporal hypometabolism concordant with MRI and EEG

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity — essential for diagnosis and lateralization
Obtain brain MRI to assess for structural etiology including hippocampal sclerosis
Order basic laboratory studies to exclude metabolic causes of seizures
Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring given equivocal findings on routine EEG
Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy after confirming epilepsy diagnosis
Recognize the clinical significance of olfactory aura (uncinate fits) as localizing to temporal lobe
contraindicated_actions: Do NOT attribute episodes to behavioral/psychiatric condition without adequate EEG evaluation
Do NOT prescribe carbamazepine without checking for HLA-B*15:02 if patient were of Southeast Asian descent (not applicable here but important principle)
Do NOT discharge without initiating antiepileptic therapy after confirmed seizure with structural lesion
Do NOT perform lumbar puncture as a first-line diagnostic test in this clinical context (no signs of infection or inflammation)
Do NOT initiate phenytoin as first-line given availability of better-tolerated modern AEDs and patient's cognitive baseline
Do NOT overlook the VP shunt — must confirm it is functioning before attributing all symptoms to epilepsy
key_reasoning_points: The semiology of behavioral arrest with oroalimentary automatisms and manual automatisms is classic for temporal lobe seizures, and the secondary generalization pattern (focal to bilateral tonic-clonic) confirms an epileptic rather than non-epileptic etiology
Olfactory aura (unpleasant smell perception) is a highly localizing symptom pointing to the temporal lobe, specifically the uncinate region — this is a subtle but critical clue in the history

The patient's complex neurological background (neonatal meningitis, VP shunt, left hemispheric injury) creates diagnostic complexity but also provides the likely etiology for mesial temporal sclerosis through the mechanism of dual pathology
Routine EEG findings are equivocal (sharp transients of uncertain significance) — recognizing the need for prolonged monitoring rather than dismissing these findings is a key clinical decision point
The breach rhythm from the VP shunt site must be recognized as an artifact and not misinterpreted as epileptiform activity
The mildly elevated TSH is a red herring — subclinical hypothyroidism with normal free T4 is not a sufficient explanation for new-onset seizures with structural correlate

Dual pathology (left hemispheric injury from neonatal meningitis + right mesial temporal sclerosis) is a well-recognized phenomenon where early brain injury leads to contralateral hippocampal sclerosis, and recognition of this pattern is important for treatment planning
The post-ictal prolactin elevation supports an epileptic seizure over a psychogenic event, though it was drawn outside the optimal window

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to equivocal initial EEG and MRI findings requiring follow-up studies, confounding neurological history (VP shunt, encephalomalacia) that complicates interpretation, red herring lab value (elevated TSH), and breach rhythm artifact that could be misinterpreted. The diagnosis requires integrating clinical semiology with multiple concordant diagnostic modalities.
